<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] CI pipeline (ci.js) lives at root alongside dev tooling, making the build contract visible without navigating into packages.
[observation] [info] Orchestration layer is correctly decoupled from all domain packages — no cross-package imports detected.
[observation] [info] Root-level service files like claude-integration.js sit outside the package boundary system; consider whether they belong in a named package.
[pattern] [warning] Orchestration-to-domain boundary is enforced at runtime only (subprocess spawning); a mismatched CLI argument or removed subcommand will produce a silent runtime failure with no compile-time safety net.
[anti-pattern] [warning] The orchestration tier's architectural boundary (no direct package imports) is enforced solely by developer convention — no ESLint rule, TypeScript path mapping, or CI check prevents a direct `import` from cli.js into a domain package. A single accidental import would collapse the tier silently.
[anti-pattern] [warning] claude-integration.js is a service file at the monorepo root that bypasses the gateway pattern entirely. Any cross-package imports it makes are invisible to gateway audits and import-graph coupling scores, creating an unmonitored coupling surface outside all four tiers of the dependency hierarchy.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Orchestration scripts (ci.js, cli.js, web.js, config.js) are kept flat at the root rather than inside a package, making the top-level entry points easy to discover.
- claude-integration.js and similar service files at the root may warrant migration into a dedicated package as the toolkit matures.
- Perfect isolation (coupling: 0) confirms this layer only spawns child CLIs and never imports from domain packages, consistent with the four-tier hierarchy.
- Orchestration layer is correctly decoupled from all domain packages — no cross-package imports detected.
- Root-level service files like claude-integration.js sit outside the package boundary system; consider whether they belong in a named package.
- CI pipeline (ci.js) lives at root alongside dev tooling, making the build contract visible without navigating into packages.
- The orchestration layer's zero coupling score is enforced only by convention (CLI spawning instead of imports), not by any compile-time mechanism — a developer could accidentally add a direct import and the coupling score would not immediately flag it as an architectural violation.
- Orchestration-to-domain boundary is enforced at runtime only (subprocess spawning); a mismatched CLI argument or removed subcommand will produce a silent runtime failure with no compile-time safety net.
- claude-integration.js is a root-level service file that sits entirely outside the package boundary and gateway system — it can import from any domain package without the cross-package import appearing in any gateway audit, making it an invisible coupling surface.
- The orchestration scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js) enforce the four-tier boundary through subprocess spawning rather than module boundaries, meaning any accidental `require()` or `import` added to these files would silently collapse the tier boundary with no lint, type, or test feedback at the point of introduction.
- claude-integration.js is a service file at the monorepo root that bypasses the gateway pattern entirely. Any cross-package imports it makes are invisible to gateway audits and import-graph coupling scores, creating an unmonitored coupling surface outside all four tiers of the dependency hierarchy.
- The orchestration tier's architectural boundary (no direct package imports) is enforced solely by developer convention — no ESLint rule, TypeScript path mapping, or CI check prevents a direct `import` from cli.js into a domain package. A single accidental import would collapse the tier silently.
- [call graph] 444 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
